source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
The study of the scattering of high energy electrons from nuclei has yielded much interesting information about the charge distributions in nuclei and nucleons. We shall here consider a simple version in which the electrons are supposed to have zero spin. We also assume that the nucleus, of charge $Z e$, remains fixed in space (i.e., its mass is assumed infinite). Let $\rho(\mathbf{x})$ denote the charge density in the nucleus. The charge distribution is assumed to be spherically symmetric but otherwise arbitrary.

Let $f_{c}\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)$, where $\mathbf{p}_{i}$ is the initial and $\mathbf{p}_{f}$ the final momentum, be the scattering amplitude in the first Born approximation for the scattering of an electron from a point-nucleus of charge $Z e$. Let $f\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)$ be the scattering amplitude of an electron from a real nucleus of the same charge. Let $\mathbf{q}=\mathbf{p}_{i}-\mathbf{p}_{f}$ denote the momentum transfer. The quantity $F$ defined by
$$ f\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)=F\left(q^{2}\right) f_{c}\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right) $$
is called the form factor. It is easily seen that F , in fact, depends on $\mathbf{p}_{i}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{f}$ only through the quantity $q^{2}$.
 Figure 2.3 shows some experimental results pertaining to the form factor for the proton, and we shall regard our theory as applicable to these data. On the basis of the data shown, compute the root-mean-square (charge) radius of the proton. Hint: Note that there is a simple relationship between the root-mean-square radius and the derivative of $F\left(q^{2}\right)$ with respect to $q^{2}$, at $q^{2}=0$. Find this relationship, and then compute.
extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object string representation rather than a concrete file path or URL

issues: metadata.image_paths contains a PIL object string representation rather than a concrete file path or URL

image_dependency: Question explicitly says 'On the basis of the data shown' and refers to 'Figure 2.3' for the proton form factor
Solving requires extracting behavior of `F(q^2)` near `q^2 = 0` from the plotted experimental data, which is not contained in the text alone
multi_step: The hint requires first deriving the relationship between the RMS radius and the derivative of `F(q^2)` at `q^2 = 0`
Then the solver must estimate the slope near the origin from the figure and convert that into a numerical proton radius
This combines physics/formula reasoning with visual data interpretation rather than a single direct lookup
verifiability: Answer is a short numeric text value (`0.77 × 10^-13 cm`), so the target format is clear
The underlying formula-based derivation is in principle checkable, but the numeric value depends on reading a slope from experimental plot data
`registry_passed` is true and the image record exists, so the asset is available for downstream verification even though plot-based estimation may allow some tolerance

We shall here consider a simple version in which the electrons are supposed to have zero spin. We also assume that the nucleus, of charge $Z e$, remains fixed in space (i.e., its mass is assumed infinite). Let $\rho(\mathbf{x})$ denote the charge density in the nucleus. The charge distribution is assumed to be spherically symmetric but otherwise arbitrary.

Let $f_{c}\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)$, where $\mathbf{p}_{i}$ is the initial and $\mathbf{p}_{f}$ the final momentum, be the scattering amplitude in the first Born approximation for the scattering of an electron from a point-nucleus of charge $Z e$. Let $f\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)$ be the scattering amplitude of an electron from a real nucleus of the same charge. Let $\mathbf{q}=\mathbf{p}_{i}-\mathbf{p}_{f}$ denote the momentum transfer. The quantity $F$ defined by
$$ f\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)=F\left(q^{2}\right) f_{c}\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right) $$
is called the form factor. It is easily seen that F , in fact, depends on $\mathbf{p}_{i}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{f}$ only through the quantity $q^{2}$.

Hint: Note that there is a simple relationship between the root-mean-square radius and the derivative of $F\left(q^{2}\right)$ with respect to $q^{2}$, at $q^{2}=0$. Hint: Note that there is a simple relationship between the root-mean-square radius and the derivative of $F\left(q^{2}\right)$ with respect to $q^{2}$, at $q^{2}=0$.
Find this relationship, and then compute.

node_records.canonical_value:
The study of the scattering of high energy electrons from nuclei has yielded much interesting information about the charge distributions in nuclei and nucleons. We shall here consider a simple version in which the electrons are supposed to have zero spin. We also assume that the nucleus, of charge $Z e$, remains fixed in space (i.e., its mass is assumed infinite). Let $\rho(\mathbf{x})$ denote the charge density in the nucleus. The charge distribution is assumed to be spherically symmetric but otherwise arbitrary.

Let $f_{c}\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)$, where $\mathbf{p}_{i}$ is the initial and $\mathbf{p}_{f}$ the final momentum, be the scattering amplitude in the first Born approximation for the scattering of an electron from a point-nucleus of charge $Z e$. Let $f\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)$ be the scattering amplitude of an electron from a real nucleus of the same charge. Let $\mathbf{q}=\mathbf{p}_{i}-\mathbf{p}_{f}$ denote the momentum transfer. The quantity $F$ defined by
$$ f\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right)=F\left(q^{2}\right) f_{c}\left(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{p}_{f}\right) $$
is called the form factor. It is easily seen that $F$, in fact, depends on $\mathbf{p}_{i}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{f}$ only through the quantity $q^{2}$. Figure 2.3 shows some experimental results pertaining to the form factor for the proton, and we shall regard our theory as applicable to these data. On the basis of the data shown, compute the root-mean-square (charge) radius of the proton. Hint: Note that there is a simple relationship between the root-mean-square radius and the derivative of $F\left(q^{2}\right)$ with respect to $q^{2}$, at $q^{2}=0$. Find this relationship, and then compute.
